preamble.summary: A psalm of David in two movements that together form one of the most celebrated poems in the Hebrew Bible. The first movement (vv. 2-7) hymns the heavens as witnesses to God's glory — the sky speaks without words, the sun runs its course like a bridegroom and a champion. The second movement (vv. 8-11) turns from creation's testimony to the Torah's perfection, cataloging the law's qualities in six parallel lines: perfect, trustworthy, right, pure, clean, true. The psalm concludes with a personal prayer (vv. 12-15) asking for cleansing from hidden faults and protection from presumptuous sins, ending with the iconic petition: 'Let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in your sight, LORD, my rock and my redeemer.'
preamble.remarkable: The junction between the two halves — from creation to Torah — is one of the most studied transitions in biblical poetry. C.S. Lewis called Psalm 19 'the greatest poem in the Psalter and one of the greatest lyrics in the world.' The connection between the two halves is not arbitrary but theological: the same God who built order into the heavens built order into the Torah. The sun that illuminates the physical world (v. 7) corresponds to the commandment that enlightens the eyes (v. 9). Creation and Torah are two forms of the same divine revelation — one written in the sky, the other on scrolls. The six descriptions of Torah in verses 8-10 form one of the most concentrated celebrations of Scripture in all of Scripture. Each line names the law by a different term (torah, edut, piqqudim, mitsvah, yirat YHVH, mishpatim), assigns it a quality (perfect, trustworthy, right, pure, clean, true), and describes its effect on the human person (restoring the soul, making the simple wise, rejoicing the heart, enlightening the eyes, enduring forever, being altogether righteous).
preamble.friction: The apparent disjunction between the creation hymn (vv. 2-7) and the Torah hymn (vv. 8-11) has led some scholars to propose that Psalm 19 was originally two separate poems joined by an editor. The creation section uses the divine name El (v. 2), while the Torah section uses YHVH (vv. 8-15) — the shift in name may reflect different sources or may be theologically deliberate (El as Creator, YHVH as covenant lawgiver). The phrase 'the fear of the LORD' (yirat YHVH, v. 10) appears in the list of Torah synonyms, which is unusual — 'fear' is a human response, not a legal corpus. Its inclusion suggests that the psalmist understands Torah not merely as legislation but as the entire relational posture of reverence toward God.
preamble.connections:
Psalm 119 is the massive expansion of what Psalm 19:8-11 compresses into six lines — the same synonyms, the same qualities, the same effects, extended over 176 verses. The creation hymn connects to Genesis 1 (creation by speech), Psalm 8 (the heavens as God's handiwork), and Romans 1:19-20 (creation revealing God's nature). Paul quotes verse 5 in Romans 10:18 to argue that the gospel has reached all nations, reading the 'voice' of creation as a type of the apostolic proclamation. The concluding prayer (v. 15) has become one of the most frequently quoted verses in Jewish and Christian liturgy.

**Tradition comparisons:** The Latin Vulgate shaped Western theology here: Caeli enarrant gloriam Dei became a foundational text for natural theology in Western tradition — the idea that creation itself reveals God. It was set to music by Haydn, Marcello, and many others. Gl... See the [Vulgate Psalms](/vulgate/psalms).

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. A psalm of David.
translator_notes: Verse 1 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: הַשָּׁמַ֗יִם מְֽסַפְּרִ֥ים כְּבוֹד־אֵ֑ל וּֽמַעֲשֵׂ֥ה יָ֝דָ֗יו מַגִּ֥יד הָרָקִֽיעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: The heavens declare the glory of God; and the firmament sheweth his handywork.
verses.rendering:
The heavens declare the glory of God,
and the expanse proclaims the work of his hands.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with what may be the most famous line in the Psalter. ha-shamayim mesapperim kevod El ('the heavens are recounting the glory of God') — the verb sapper means 'to count, recount, narrate, tell.' The heavens are not silent but speaking — engaged in continuous narration (the participle indicates ongoing action). What they narrate is kavod El — the 'glory of God,' the weight, splendor, and radiance of the Creator made visible in what he created. The parallel line adds: u-ma'aseh yadav maggid ha-raqia ('and the work of his hands the expanse declares'). The raqia ('expanse, firmament') — the same word from Genesis 1:6 — proclaims (maggid, from nagad, 'to declare, announce') that it was made by divine hands. The heavens are a text; creation is a proclamation.
translator_notes: kavod ('glory') when applied to God refers to the visible manifestation of his presence — weight, substance, radiance that can be perceived. The heavens do not merely suggest God's existence; they actively narrate his kavod, making the invisible God's attributes perceptible.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, splendor, radiance, the visible manifestation of divine presence
key_terms.note: kavod derives from kaved ('to be heavy, weighty'). God's glory is his substance made perceptible — not decoration but density. When the heavens declare God's kavod, they reveal something real about who God is, not merely that he exists.

verses.text_hebrew: י֣וֹם לְ֭יוֹם יַבִּ֣יעַ אֹ֑מֶר וְלַ֥יְלָה לְּ֝לַ֗יְלָה יְחַוֶּה־דָּֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: Day unto day uttereth speech, and night unto night sheweth knowledge.
verses.rendering:
Day after day pours out speech,
and night after night reveals knowledge.
verses.expanded_rendering: The heavenly testimony is relentless and continuous. yom le-yom yabbiya omer ('day to day pours forth speech') — the verb nava ('to pour out, bubble up, gush') pictures speech overflowing like a spring that cannot be stopped. The word omer ('speech, utterance') indicates articulate communication, not mere noise. ve-lailah le-lailah yechavveh da'at ('and night to night declares knowledge') — the verb chavah means 'to declare, show, make known.' Each day passes its testimony to the next day; each night hands its knowledge to the following night. The image is of an unbroken chain of witness — creation is a conversation that never pauses, a relay of proclamation running through all time.
translator_notes: The personification of day and night as speakers who pass testimony to one another creates a remarkable image: time itself is a medium of revelation. Every 24-hour cycle is a fresh installment in creation's ongoing sermon about God.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֣ין אֹ֭מֶר וְאֵ֣ין דְּבָרִ֑ים בְּ֝לִ֗י נִשְׁמָ֥ע קוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: There is no speech nor language, where their voice is not heard.
verses.rendering:
There is no speech and there are no words;
their voice is not heard.
translator_notes: This verse can be read two ways: 'There is no speech... [yet] their voice goes out' (the paradox of wordless communication) or 'There is no speech or language where their voice is not heard' (universal reach). The first reading is more consistent with the Hebrew syntax and creates the more powerful theological point.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּכׇל־הָאָ֨רֶץ ׀ יָ֘צָ֤א קַוָּ֗ם וּבִקְצֵ֣ה תֵ֭בֵל מִלֵּיהֶ֑ם לַ֝שֶּׁ֗מֶשׁ שָֽׂם־אֹ֥הֶל בָּהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Their line is gone out through all the earth, and their words to the end of the world. In them hath he set a tabernacle for the sun,
verses.rendering:
Yet their measuring line goes out through all the earth,
and their words to the end of the world.
In them he has set a tent for the sun,
verses.expanded_rendering: Despite having no audible voice, creation's testimony reaches everywhere. be-khol ha-arets yatsa qavvam ('through all the earth their line has gone out') — the word qav means 'line, measuring cord' — the same surveyor's line used to measure property. Creation's witness has been measured out across the entire earth; no territory is unclaimed. u-vi-qtseh tevel millehem ('and to the end of the world their words'). Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:18, reading the 'line' of creation's testimony as a type of the gospel's universal proclamation. The verse then pivots to the psalm's central image: la-shemesh sam ohel bahem ('for the sun he has set a tent among them'). God has pitched a tent (ohel) for the sun in the heavens — a dwelling from which the sun emerges each morning.
translator_notes: qav ('line, measuring cord') is read by the Septuagint as qol ('voice'), producing 'their voice has gone out through all the earth.' Paul follows the Septuagint reading in Romans 10:18. The Hebrew qav ('measuring line') suggests that creation's witness has a precise, measured extent — it reaches everywhere by design, not by accident.

verses.text_hebrew: וְה֗וּא כְּ֭חָתָן יֹצֵ֣א מֵחֻפָּת֑וֹ יָשִׂ֥ישׂ כְּ֝גִבּ֗וֹר לָר֥וּץ אֹֽרַח׃
verses.text_kjv: Which is as a bridegroom coming out of his chamber, and rejoiceth as a strong man to run a race.
verses.rendering:
It comes out like a bridegroom from his chamber,
rejoicing like a champion to run its course.
verses.expanded_rendering: The sun is personified with two vivid images. First: ke-chatan yotse me-chuppato ('like a bridegroom coming out of his wedding canopy'). The chuppah is the bridal chamber or wedding tent — the sun emerges at dawn with the radiance and joy of a groom on his wedding morning. The image is of beauty, vitality, and anticipation. Second: yasis ke-gibbor laruts orach ('it rejoices like a warrior to run its path'). The gibbor is a mighty man, a champion warrior, and the sun's daily journey across the sky is a race run with exuberant strength. Together the images combine beauty (bridegroom) with power (warrior), tenderness with strength. The sun is both lovely and unstoppable.
translator_notes: The dual imagery — bridegroom and warrior — resists any reduction of the sun to mere metaphor. The psalmist sees the sun as genuinely magnificent, a creation that reflects its Creator's glory through both beauty and power. Ancient Near Eastern cultures worshipped the sun as a deity; the psalmist demotes it to a tent-dweller (v. 5) who runs a course God assigned.

verses.text_hebrew: מִקְצֵ֤ה הַשָּׁמַ֨יִם ׀ מ֘וֹצָא֗וֹ וּתְקוּפָת֥וֹ עַל־קְצוֹתָ֑ם וְאֵ֥ין נִ֝סְתָּ֗ר מֵחַמָּתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: His going forth is from the end of the heaven, and his circuit unto the ends of it: and there is nothing hid from the heat thereof.
verses.expanded_rendering: The sun's course spans the entire sky: mi-qtseh ha-shamayim motsa'o ('from the end of the heavens is its going forth') u-tequfato al qetsotam ('and its circuit reaches to their ends'). The scope is total — horizon to horizon, the complete arc. The final clause serves as the bridge between the psalm's two halves: ve-ein nistar me-chammato ('and nothing is hidden from its heat'). The word chammah ('heat, warmth') reaches everything — no shadow, no hiding place, no corner of the earth escapes the sun's penetrating warmth. This line anticipates the Torah section: just as nothing is hidden from the sun's heat, nothing in the human heart is hidden from Torah's searching light. The sun illuminates the physical world; Torah illuminates the moral one.
translator_notes: The phrase 'nothing is hidden from its heat' functions as a literary hinge. In the creation section, it refers to the sun's physical reach. Read in light of the Torah section that follows, it becomes a statement about the comprehensiveness of divine revelation: God's word, like God's sun, penetrates everywhere.

verses.text_hebrew: תּ֘וֹרַ֤ת יְהוָ֣ה תְּ֭מִימָה מְשִׁ֣יבַת נָ֑פֶשׁ עֵד֥וּת יְ֝הוָ֗ה נֶ֭אֱמָנָה מַחְכִּ֣ימַת פֶּֽתִי׃
verses.text_kjv: The law of the LORD is perfect, converting the soul: the testimony of the LORD is sure, making wise the simple.
verses.rendering:
The law of the LORD is perfect,
restoring the soul.
The testimony of the LORD is trustworthy,
making the simple wise.
verses.expanded_rendering: The Torah section begins, and the divine name shifts from El (v. 2) to YHVH — the covenant God now speaks not through creation but through Torah. The first pair: torat YHVH temimah meshivat nafesh ('the Torah of the LORD is perfect, restoring the soul'). Torah means 'instruction, teaching, law' — from the root yarah ('to cast, throw, direct, instruct'). It is temimah ('perfect, complete, without defect') — the same word used for unblemished sacrificial animals and for the integrity of the righteous (Psalm 15:2). Its effect: it restores (meshivat, from shuv, 'to return, restore') the nefesh ('soul, life, being'). Torah brings the depleted self back to wholeness. The second pair: edut YHVH ne'emanah machkimat peti ('the testimony of the LORD is reliable, making the naive wise'). The edut ('testimony, witness, decree') is ne'emanah ('trustworthy, firm, reliable' — from the root aman, source of 'amen'). Its effect: it takes the peti ('simple one, naive, inexperienced') and makes them wise (chakham). Torah does not merely inform; it transforms.
translator_notes: The six Torah lines (vv. 8-10) form a tightly structured catalog. Each line names the law (torah, edut, piqqudim, mitsvah, yirat YHVH, mishpatim), describes its character (perfect, trustworthy, right, pure, clean, true), and states its effect on the person who receives it (restoring, making wise, rejoicing, enlightening, enduring, being righteous).

key_terms.semantic_range: instruction, teaching, law, direction, guidance — from yarah ('to direct, instruct, throw')
key_terms.note: torah is broader than 'law' in the English sense. It encompasses instruction, guidance, and teaching — the full range of God's communicated will. Rendering it as 'law' captures its binding authority but may obscure its character as loving instruction from a wise teacher.

key_terms.semantic_range: complete, whole, without defect, sound, blameless
key_terms.note: temimah applied to Torah means it lacks nothing — it is complete in its guidance, flawless in its design, and sufficient for its purpose. The same word describes Noah's character (Genesis 6:9) and the required condition of a sacrifice (Leviticus 1:3).

verses.text_hebrew: פִּקּ֘וּדֵ֤י יְהוָ֣ה יְ֭שָׁרִים מְשַׂמְּחֵי־לֵ֑ב מִצְוַ֥ת יְ֝הוָ֗ה בָּ֭רָה מְאִירַ֣ת עֵינָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: The statutes of the LORD are right, rejoicing the heart: the commandment of the LORD is pure, enlightening the eyes.
verses.rendering:
The precepts of the LORD are right,
rejoicing the heart.
The commandment of the LORD is pure,
enlightening the eyes.
verses.expanded_rendering: The third pair: piqqudei YHVH yesharim mesamchei lev ('the precepts of the LORD are straight, causing the heart to rejoice'). piqqudim ('precepts, orders, detailed instructions') are yashar ('straight, right, upright'). Their effect is joy — they make the heart glad. This counters the assumption that divine instruction is burdensome: Torah properly received produces simchah ('joy, gladness'). The fourth pair: mitsvat YHVH barah me'irat einayim ('the commandment of the LORD is clear/pure, making the eyes shine'). mitsvah ('commandment') is barah ('clear, clean, radiant'). Its effect: it enlightens (me'irat, from or, 'light') the eyes. This echoes the sun imagery of verses 5-7 — just as the sun illuminates the world, God's commandment illuminates perception. Torah is the moral sun that lets people see clearly.
translator_notes: The claim that Torah rejoices the heart is not peripheral but central to the psalm's theology. God's instruction is not a burden but a delight. This is the same conviction that drives Psalm 119's repeated expressions of love for the law.

key_terms.semantic_range: commandment, order, charge, what is commanded
key_terms.note: mitsvah (from tsavah, 'to command') is the most direct term for a divine order. It implies authority and obligation. The psalm says this commanding word is barah ('pure, clear') — not arbitrary or obscure but luminous.

verses.text_hebrew: יִרְאַ֤ת יְהוָ֨ה ׀ טְהוֹרָה֮ עוֹמֶ֢דֶת לָ֫עַ֥ד מִשְׁפְּטֵ֥י יְהוָ֥ה אֱמֶ֑ת צָֽדְק֥וּ יַחְדָּֽו׃
verses.text_kjv: The fear of the LORD is clean, enduring for ever: the judgments of the LORD are true and righteous altogether.
verses.rendering:
The fear of the LORD is clean,
standing forever.
The judgments of the LORD are true,
righteous altogether.
verses.expanded_rendering: The fifth pair: yirat YHVH tehorah omedet la-ad ('the fear of the LORD is clean, standing forever'). yirah ('fear, reverence, awe') is unusual in this catalog — it is a human response, not a textual corpus. Its inclusion suggests that the psalmist understands Torah and the fear of God as inseparable: Torah is not merely a set of instructions but an encounter with God that produces awe. This fear is tehorah ('clean, pure') and omedet la-ad ('standing forever, enduring for all time') — it does not decay, rust, or become obsolete. The sixth pair: mishpetei YHVH emet tsadequ yachdav ('the judgments of the LORD are truth; they are righteous together'). mishpatim ('judgments, ordinances, legal decisions') are emet ('truth, reliability, faithfulness'). And they are righteous yachdav ('altogether, without exception'). Not some of God's judgments are right — all of them, taken together, form a unified system of justice.
translator_notes: The inclusion of yirat YHVH ('fear of the LORD') in a list of Torah synonyms is theologically significant. It identifies the reverential posture toward God as itself a form of Torah — perhaps the foundational form from which all specific commandments flow. Proverbs 1:7 calls this fear 'the beginning of knowledge.'

key_terms.semantic_range: reverence, awe, fear, worship, the foundational posture of creature before Creator
key_terms.note: yirah ('fear') of God is not terror but the appropriate human response to encountering the holy. It is called 'clean' (tehorah) because it purifies rather than contaminates, and 'enduring forever' because unlike human institutions, this reverence never becomes obsolete.

key_terms.semantic_range: truth, faithfulness, reliability, firmness, what corresponds to reality
key_terms.note: emet derives from the same root as emunah ('faithfulness') and amen. When the psalm says God's judgments are emet, it means they correspond to reality — they are not arbitrary rules but accurate descriptions of how the moral universe works.

verses.text_hebrew: הַנֶּחֱמָדִ֗ים מִ֭זָּהָב וּמִפַּ֣ז רָ֑ב וּמְתוּקִ֥ים מִ֝דְּבַ֗שׁ וְנֹ֣פֶת צוּפִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: More to be desired are they than gold, yea, than much fine gold: sweeter also than honey and the honeycomb.
verses.rendering:
More desirable than gold — than much fine gold;
sweeter than honey — than honey dripping from the comb.
translator_notes: The comparison of Torah to gold addresses value; the comparison to honey addresses pleasure. Together they insist that God's instruction is both the most valuable possession and the most enjoyable experience available to a human being. This is not dutiful obedience but passionate desire.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־עַ֭בְדְּךָ נִזְהָ֣ר בָּהֶ֑ם בְּ֝שׇׁמְרָ֗ם עֵ֣קֶב רָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Moreover by them is thy servant warned: and in keeping of them there is great reward.
verses.rendering:
Moreover, by them your servant is warned;
in keeping them there is great reward.
translator_notes: The psalmist now identifies himself as avdekha ('your servant') — the same title given to David in the superscription of Psalm 18. The reward (eqev rav, 'great consequence/reward') is not specified — it may be the very qualities listed in verses 8-9 (restored soul, wise mind, glad heart, enlightened eyes) rather than external prosperity.

verses.text_kjv: Who can understand his errors? cleanse thou me from secret faults.
verses.rendering:
Who can discern their errors?
Cleanse me from hidden faults.
translator_notes: The movement from Torah's perfection (vv. 8-11) to human imperfection (vv. 13-14) is not a contradiction but a consequence. The more clearly one sees the law's perfection, the more clearly one sees one's own failure. Torah's light reveals what was hidden.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּ֤ם מִזֵּדִ֨ים ׀ חֲשֹׂ֬ךְ עַבְדֶּ֗ךָ אַֽל־יִמְשְׁלוּ־בִ֥י אָ֣ז אֵ֭יתָם וְנִקֵּ֣יתִי מִפֶּ֣שַׁע רָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Keep back thy servant also from presumptuous sins; let them not have dominion over me: then shall I be upright, and I shall be innocent from the great transgression.
verses.rendering:
Also keep your servant from willful sins;
do not let them rule over me.
Then I will be blameless,
and innocent of great transgression.
verses.expanded_rendering: From hidden faults (v. 13) the psalmist moves to deliberate sins: gam mi-zedim chasokh avdekha ('also from presumptuous/willful acts restrain your servant'). The zedim ('arrogant, presumptuous, willful acts') are the opposite of shegi'ot ('errors'): these are conscious, deliberate transgressions committed with full knowledge. The psalmist asks God to restrain him — the verb chasakh means 'to hold back, withhold, restrain.' He does not trust his own willpower but asks God to prevent him from sinning deliberately. al yimshelu vi ('do not let them rule over me') — the language of dominion (mashal, 'to rule') treats willful sin as a potential master. Sin is not merely an act but a power that seeks to govern. If God restrains the servant from presumption, then: eitam ve-niqqeti mi-pesha rav ('I will be blameless and clean of great transgression'). The pesha rav ('great transgression') may refer to apostasy, to a definitive turning away from God — the ultimate sin from which the psalmist prays to be preserved.
translator_notes: The progression from hidden faults (unconscious) to willful sins (conscious) to great transgression (definitive) maps an escalating hierarchy of sin. The psalmist asks for cleansing at every level — from the faults he cannot see to the rebellion he fears he might choose.

verses.text_hebrew: יִהְי֥וּ לְרָצ֨וֹן ׀ אִמְרֵ֘י פִ֤י וְהֶגְי֣וֹן לִ֭בִּי לְפָנֶ֑יךָ יְ֝הוָ֗ה צוּרִ֥י וְגֹאֲלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Let the words of my mouth, and the meditation of my heart, be acceptable in thy sight, O LORD, my strength, and my redeemer.
verses.rendering:
Let the words of my mouth
and the meditation of my heart
be acceptable before you,
LORD, my rock and my Redeemer.
translator_notes: The go'el ('redeemer') is a legal term from Israelite family law. The go'el was the nearest kinsman responsible for buying back family land that had been sold, redeeming a relative from slavery, or avenging a murdered family member (Leviticus 25:25, Ruth 3:12-13). By calling God go'ali, the psalmist claims God as his closest family member — the one with both the obligation and the power to buy him back.

key_terms.semantic_range: redeemer, kinsman-redeemer, one who buys back, one who restores what was lost
key_terms.note: The go'el is the family member who rescues a relative from loss, debt, or danger. Calling God go'eli ('my redeemer') makes the most intimate human relationship — kin obligation — a metaphor for divine salvation. God is not a distant deity but the nearest relative, bound by love to buy back what the psalmist has lost.

key_terms.semantic_range: rock, cliff, boulder, bedrock — metaphorically: stability, permanence, refuge
key_terms.note: tsur as a divine title appears throughout the Psalms and in Deuteronomy 32 (the Song of Moses). It communicates absolute reliability — God as the geological foundation that does not shift, crack, or erode.
